one_line_takeaway: By decomposing source-to-sink vulnerability detection into deterministic per-edge subtasks with pruned/simplified context rather than letting an LLM agent freely explore, VULSOLVER raises OWASP Benchmark accuracy from CodeQL's 52-57% and prior LLM-agent baselines' 48-74% to 97.85% (100% recall, 96.02% precision), and both ablations show this gain is driven almost entirely by precision (false-positive suppression), not recall.
key_finding: On the full OWASP Benchmark, VULSOLVER (Kimi-K2) achieves 97.85% accuracy, 96.02% precision, 100% recall, and 97.97% F1 (531 TP, 22 FP, 470 TN, 0 FN). Per-vulnerability-type F1: command injection 98.44% (98.41% acc), SQL injection 98.37% (98.21% acc), path traversal 96.73% (96.64% acc, the hardest category). Against baselines on accuracy, CodeQL scores 52-57%, CWE-DF 48-52%, and Best-Rated Agent 67.8-74.3% across the three vulnerability types -- the paper reports VULSOLVER's accuracy improvement over baselines is 'at least 24%'. Across LLM backbones, Kimi-K2 is best for command injection (98.41% acc, vs. DeepSeek-V3 93.63%, GPT-4o 90.44%, GPT-4-Turbo 92.83%) and SQL injection (98.21% acc), but DeepSeek-V3 outperforms Kimi-K2 specifically on path traversal (98.88% acc vs 96.64%). Ablation: removing Branch Method Analysis drops overall accuracy from 97.85% to 64.42% and precision from 96.02% to 59.33% (100% recall preserved across all ablations); removing Context Maintenance drops overall accuracy to 78.09% and precision to 69.96%, with the Path Traversal category hit hardest (precision falls to 62.44%). The authors interpret the uniform 100% recall across ablations as evidence that LLMs readily flag candidate vulnerabilities but need the two pruning/context modules to recognize when code is actually non-exploitable (i.e., the modules primarily suppress false positives, not false negatives). In the real-world case study across three open-source projects, VULSOLVER found 15 confirmed, previously unknown vulnerabilities (6 command injection, 4 path traversal, 5 SQL injection) with estimated CVSS scores of 7.5-9.8; the authors state they submitted reports to the maintainers and are pursuing CVE assignments.
core_contribution: VULSOLVER formalizes source-to-sink vulnerability detection as a constraint-satisfaction problem over a call chain, defined by two constraint types: Transfer Constraints (the sink is reachable from the source along a feasible execution path) and Trigger Constraints (the arguments reaching the sink satisfy the conditions needed for exploitation, e.g. an unfiltered '..' in a path parameter). Rather than giving an LLM agent free rein over the whole codebase, the system decomposes the problem into a deterministic sequence of subtasks, one per adjacent caller-callee pair on the SAST-extracted main path, where each subtask uses an LLM only to derive the callee's parameter 'exploitability state' from the caller's state plus tightly scoped context. Three modules implement each subtask: (1) Branch Method Analysis, which prunes each branch method's call tree (via data-flow filtering, BFS layer filtering, known-semantics filtering, and an upper size limit) and asks the LLM to extract only the semantic 'objective' relevant to exploitability (e.g. which parameters reach a critical-type return value unfiltered); (2) Context Maintenance, which prunes irrelevant parameters/branch methods and simplifies retained descriptions (propagation, filtering, and exploitability-judgment simplification) into a compact textual summary; and (3) Main Path Analysis, which feeds the LLM only the caller's own source code plus this distilled context to infer the callee's parameter state. States propagate along the main path until the sink is reached, at which point the sink's derived parameter states are checked against the Trigger Constraints. A SAST tool (Tabby) is used purely as an information provider (extracting call chains and a structured 'Code Information Summary' with method signatures, caller-callee relationships, critical-type parameters, and taint propagation) rather than as the final decision-maker, so paths SAST would itself reject as unexploitable are still passed to the LLM stage.
threat_model: The paper targets injection-class vulnerabilities reachable via source-to-sink call chains in application source code: command injection, path traversal, and SQL injection (the three OWASP Benchmark categories used in evaluation). The adversary is an external attacker supplying malicious input (e.g. an HTTP request parameter) that an insufficiently sanitized code path propagates to a dangerous sink (file write/read, OS command execution, SQL query construction). This is a defensive/detection threat model -- the paper does not attack or red-team an LLM, it uses an LLM as an analysis engine to find vulnerabilities in third-party code before attackers do. Real-world validation targeted three open-source Java projects (PublicCMS, JeeWMS, dreamer-cms).
limitations: The paper does not report false-positive/false-negative counts for the real-world case study (only the 15 confirmed vulnerabilities are stated; the rate of SAST-flagged candidate paths that were rejected after LLM+manual review is not given, so precision/recall in the wild cannot be assessed). CodeQL, CWE-DF, and Best-Rated Agent numbers are taken from prior published studies rather than re-run under identical conditions in this paper, which is a threat to comparability (different code/version, possibly different sample subsets). The evaluation is restricted to three vulnerability classes (command injection, path traversal, SQL injection) that share a common source-to-sink taint pattern; the constraint-solving formulation's applicability to vulnerability classes without a clean single-sink taint model (e.g. race conditions, business-logic flaws, use-after-free) is not evaluated. All main-path extraction depends on the underlying SAST tool (Tabby) correctly enumerating candidate call chains; the paper does not quantify SAST's own recall for missing candidate paths entirely (SAST is explicitly used to over-include paths, but no metric bounds how many true vulnerable paths SAST might still omit). Cost/latency of the multi-subtask LLM pipeline versus a single-pass LLM call is not reported.

datasets_used: OWASP Benchmark (1,023 labeled samples: 531 vulnerable, 492 benign; command injection, path traversal, SQL injection)
Three real-world open-source Java projects: PublicCMS, JeeWMS, dreamer-cms
quantitative_results: Overall VULSOLVER (Kimi-K2) on OWASP: 97.85% accuracy, 96.02% precision, 100% recall, 97.97% F1 (531 TP, 22 FP, 470 TN, 0 FN)
Command Injection: 98.41% acc, 96.92% prec, 100% rec, 98.44% F1
Path Traversal: 96.64% acc, 93.66% prec, 100% rec, 96.73% F1
SQL Injection: 98.21% acc, 96.80% prec, 100% rec, 98.37% F1
VULSOLVER (DeepSeek-V3) Path Traversal: 98.88% acc, 97.79% prec, 100% rec, 98.88% F1 (best of all backbone/type combos)
VULSOLVER (GPT-4o) overall range across types: 90.44-92.86% acc
VULSOLVER (GPT-4-Turbo) overall range across types: 92.83-94.03% acc
Ablation w/o Branch Method Analysis (overall): 64.42% acc, 59.33% prec, 100% rec, 74.47% F1
Ablation w/o Context Maintenance (overall): 78.09% acc, 69.96% prec, 100% rec, 82.33% F1 (Path Traversal precision falls to 62.44%)
Real-world case study: 15 confirmed previously unknown vulnerabilities (6 command injection, 4 path traversal, 5 SQL injection), estimated CVSS 7.5-9.8
baselines_compared: CodeQL (industrial SAST): 52-57% accuracy, 50-54% precision, 77-100% recall across the three vulnerability types (numbers from prior cited study)
CWE-DF (best LLM+prompting configuration from prior study): 48-52% accuracy, 48-52% precision, 100% recall
Best-Rated Agent (best AI agent from prior study): 67.8-74.3% accuracy (precision/recall not reported in the original study)
